student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: Numbers That Speak -- Talking About Data and Research Findings
lesson.menu_desc: Talking about numbers, statistics, and research findings in everyday conversation -- how to read data, describe trends, and share results clearly -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 17 -- Numbers That Speak
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 17 -- Numbers That Speak
lesson.grammar_point: talking about numbers, quantities and statistics

listenings.text: Good morning, everyone. Let me share a few numbers from our latest survey on reading habits. We asked roughly two thousand people how often they read for fun. The data shows a clear trend. About sixty percent of adults read at least one book a month, and that figure has increased slightly since last year. Interestingly, the average person now spends around twenty minutes a day reading on a screen. The rate is higher among younger people. One in three said they read mostly on their phones. The results suggest that people are not reading less; they are simply reading differently. We estimate that this trend will continue over the next few years. Those are the main figures. Thank you for listening.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sofia! I just read an interesting article and I have to tell you the numbers. So, a big survey looked at how much people sleep. On average, adults sleep about six and a half hours a night, which is less than the seven or eight hours doctors recommend. The percentage of people who feel tired during the day has increased a lot in the last ten years. Honestly, the figures surprised me. One in four adults said they rarely feel rested. But there was some good news too: the number of people who exercise regularly is going up, roughly a five percent increase since last year. The article estimated that small changes, like a short walk, really help. Anyway, call me back and tell me what you think as a doctor. Bye!

extra_audio.text: First, say what the data is about and where the numbers come from. Then give the main figure, like a percentage or an average. After that, describe the trend and say if the number went up or down. Next, add one more result that supports your point. Finally, estimate what might happen next or explain why the numbers matter.
